nutrient_source.title: Passionfruit Green Tea (No Topping) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for Passionfruit Green Tea (No Topping), cr_id D08010083. All per-100g values preserved as reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — sugar-sweetened tea beverages analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for passionfruit-flavoured green tea. GI estimated at 55 based on sugar-sweetened tea beverages in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The beverage contains 7.49 g sugar/100 g (predominantly sucrose/fructose from passionfruit flavouring) with negligible fibre. Per the standard GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70), a GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category. GL per 250 g serving = (55 × 18.73 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 10.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage — 7.49 g sugar/100 g; sodium is low at 1 mg/100 g but liquid sugar independently elevates blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage; the 7.49 g/100 g sugar load independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, and an unsweetened or zero-sugar version should be requested instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage — 7.49 g sugar/100 g with zero dietary fibre; liquid sugar causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage as it is a sugar-sweetened drink delivering 7.49 g sugar/100 g with no fibre buffer, causing acute postprandial blood glucose spikes; an unsweetened green tea variant is strongly preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased cardiovascular disease risk; fat and sodium are negligible but sugar load is clinically relevant
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should limit or avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage, as habitual SSB consumption is independently associated with increased CVD incidence per meta-analyses; switching to unsweetened green tea preserves the cardioprotective EGCG polyphenol benefit without the sugar burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar at 7.49 g/100 g with zero fibre promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; green tea EGCG is present but insufficient to offset liquid sugar inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should switch to an unsweetened passionfruit green tea to access EGCG anti-inflammatory polyphenol benefits without the pro-inflammatory effect of 7.49 g/100 g liquid sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Passionfruit Green Tea (No Topping) is a freshly brewed green tea beverage flavoured with passionfruit syrup or concentrate, served without any customisable toppings such as pearls, jelly, or pudding. It is a popular speciality tea drink widely available at Singaporean bubble tea chains and hawker-adjacent beverage stalls. The drink is light amber-green in colour with a floral, tropical-fruity aroma and a mildly sweet, slightly tangy taste derived from the passionfruit flavouring. The absence of toppings reduces total caloric and carbohydrate load compared to topped variants, but the beverage base remains a sugar-sweetened drink. Per 100 g it provides 29 kcal, 7.49 g carbohydrate (all as sugar), 0.01 g protein, 0 g fat, 0 g dietary fibre, and 1 mg sodium. The high sugar-to-fibre ratio (7.49 g sugar with zero fibre) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease risk. Green tea polyphenols (EGCG) are present and confer antioxidant properties, but these benefits are substantially offset by the liquid sugar content. An unsweetened version of this drink would be a significantly healthier alternative. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low, at the upper boundary of the low GI range) based on analogous sugar-sweetened tea beverages, with a glycemic load of approximately 10.3 per 250 ml serving.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08010083) with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record. HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean food items. All per-100g values are preserved exactly as reported.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database for this beverage formulation — this is a water-based brewed tea with no fat-containing ingredients. saturated_fat_g and cholesterol_mg are confirmed zero for the same reason. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB; the beverage is a liquid with no intact plant fibre. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown for this specific flavoured green tea beverage is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026. GI value of 55 is an analogous estimate based on sugar-sweetened tea beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for passionfruit green tea was found in approved sources. GI 55 is classified as 'low' per the standard scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70); the previous iteration incorrectly assigned 'medium' — corrected in this iteration. Ontology codes use closest defensible approximations: FoodOn green tea beverage class and FoodEx2 A0FHR (liquid green tea); no passionfruit-flavoured variant codes exist in either system.
